course_info.title: Public Relations in Digital Era
prerequisites: DC201 - Social Media Strategy and Management
DC402 - Crisis Communication in Digital Age

class_schedule.topic: Modern Public Relations: From Gatekeepers to Direct Access
learning_objectives: Explain how digital channels have transformed the public relations profession
Map the modern media landscape including traditional outlets, digital publications, podcasts, and social media
Identify the core functions of PR: media relations, reputation management, stakeholder communication, and thought leadership
readings: Breakenridge, Social Media and Public Relations, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Public relations is no longer about press releases and media gatekeepers. Digital channels give organizations direct access to every stakeholder, and every stakeholder has a megaphone. This course teaches modern PR that integrates earned, owned, and shared media into cohesive reputation strategies.
key_concepts: The PESO model: Paid, Earned, Shared, Owned media integration in modern PR
Media landscape evolution: legacy media declining but still influential, digital-native publications rising, podcasts and newsletters as new earned media
Direct-to-audience communication: owned channels reduce dependency on media intermediaries
Thought leadership: building executive and organizational authority through content and visibility
Reputation as an asset: PR's role in building, protecting, and recovering organizational reputation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a successful PR campaign that integrated all PESO channels, mapping each tactic to the model and measuring the contribution of each media type to overall campaign impact

review.value: Paid (advertising, sponsored content), Earned (media coverage, reviews), Shared (social media, user-generated content), Owned (website, blog, email); modern PR integrates all four rather than focusing solely on earned media

Positioning executives or the organization as authoritative voices in their field through bylined articles, speaking engagements, research publication, and social media presence, building credibility that supports all PR objectives

class_schedule.topic: Media Relations in the Digital Age
learning_objectives: Write media pitches that earn coverage in digital-first and traditional outlets
Build and maintain a media contact list with appropriate relationship management
Adapt PR materials for journalists, bloggers, podcasters, and social media influencers
readings: Breakenridge, Social Media and Public Relations, Ch. 5-7
Muck Rack, 'The State of Journalism' (annual survey)

lecture_notes.introduction: Journalists receive hundreds of pitches daily and ignore most of them. The pitches that succeed offer genuine news value, demonstrate understanding of the journalist's beat, and make the reporter's job easier. Digital tools have changed how pitches are delivered but not the fundamentals of what makes them effective.
key_concepts: The pitch: a concise, personalized email proposing a story to a specific journalist based on their beat and interests
News value criteria: timeliness, impact, proximity, prominence, novelty, conflict, human interest
Media list building: researching journalists by beat, outlet, and coverage history using tools like Muck Rack and Cision
Digital press kit: online newsroom with press releases, high-resolution images, executive bios, fact sheets, and B-roll
Pitching beyond traditional media: podcast booking, newsletter features, blog guest posts, and influencer outreach
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing three media pitches for the same announcement: one for a national news outlet, one for an industry trade publication, and one for a relevant podcast, tailoring angle, tone, and format for each

review.value: A brief, personalized email (under 200 words) that proposes a story angle to a specific journalist, demonstrating familiarity with their work, offering a clear news hook, and making the next step easy (interview, data, visual assets)

An online newsroom accessible to journalists 24/7, containing press releases, high-resolution logos and images, executive bios and headshots, company fact sheet, and contact information, eliminating back-and-forth requests for basic assets

learning_objectives: Apply digital PR techniques to earn high-authority backlinks for SEO impact
Create data-driven content designed to attract media coverage and links
Develop newsjacking strategies that insert the brand into trending conversations responsibly
readings: Enge et al., The Art of SEO, Ch. 9 - Link Earning Through PR
BuzzSumo, 'Content Marketing and PR Integration' guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital PR merges traditional media relations with SEO strategy. When a journalist links to your research, report, or resource in their article, you earn both the credibility of media coverage and the SEO authority of a high-quality backlink. This convergence makes digital PR one of the most valuable communications disciplines.
key_concepts: Digital PR defined: earning online media coverage that includes backlinks to your site
Data-driven PR: commissioning original research, surveys, and data analysis that journalists want to cite
Newsjacking: inserting your brand's expertise into trending news stories as a source or commentator
Visual PR: creating infographics, interactive tools, and data visualizations that earn media embeds
Measuring digital PR: media coverage + backlink quality + referral traffic + search ranking impact
lecture_notes.demonstration: Developing a data-driven digital PR campaign: identifying a research angle, designing a survey, creating a visual asset, writing the press release, and building a targeted media list for outreach
setup_requirements: Survey design tool (Google Forms or Typeform)

review.value: The practice of quickly inserting your brand's expertise or perspective into breaking news or trending topics to earn media coverage and social visibility; requires speed, relevance, and genuine expertise to avoid appearing opportunistic

Creating original research (surveys, data analysis, trend reports) that provides journalists with fresh data to cite in their stories, earning both coverage and authoritative backlinks that benefit SEO

learning_objectives: Develop stakeholder communication plans addressing diverse audience needs
Write corporate communications including press releases, executive statements, and internal announcements
Manage multi-stakeholder messaging when different audiences require different information
readings: PR News, 'Best Practices in Corporate Communications'

lecture_notes.introduction: Organizations communicate with multiple stakeholders simultaneously: employees, customers, investors, regulators, communities, and the public. Each group has different information needs, different levels of knowledge, and different expectations. Effective PR manages this complexity without contradiction.
key_concepts: Stakeholder mapping: identifying all stakeholder groups, their information needs, preferred channels, and influence level
Message architecture: a hierarchy of messages tailored to each stakeholder while maintaining consistency
Press release writing: the inverted pyramid, news hook, quotes, boilerplate, and multimedia elements
Internal communications: employees as the most important audience and first line of brand advocacy
Investor and regulatory communications: precision, compliance, and managed disclosure requirements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Developing a multi-stakeholder communication plan for a product recall: drafting distinct but consistent messages for customers, employees, media, regulators, and investors, ensuring factual consistency across all

review.value: A visual framework plotting stakeholders by their level of interest and influence, enabling prioritized communication: high-influence/high-interest stakeholders receive personalized, proactive communication; low-influence/low-interest stakeholders receive standard information

A structured framework where a core message is adapted for each stakeholder group: the facts remain consistent but the framing, emphasis, and call to action change based on what each audience needs to know and do

learning_objectives: Apply the Barcelona Principles to measure PR impact beyond clip counting
Calculate and report on share of voice, message penetration, and sentiment
Build a PR measurement dashboard connecting activities to organizational outcomes

lecture_notes.introduction: PR measurement has evolved far beyond counting press clippings and calculating advertising value equivalency (AVE). The Barcelona Principles establish a global standard for meaningful PR measurement that connects communications activity to business outcomes.
key_concepts: Barcelona Principles: goal setting, measuring communication outcomes not just outputs, moving beyond AVE
PR outputs vs. outcomes vs. impact: press releases sent (output), coverage achieved (outcome), behavior change (impact)
Share of voice: your brand's proportion of industry media coverage and conversation
Sentiment analysis: tracking whether coverage and conversation about your brand is positive, negative, or neutral
Business impact metrics: connecting PR activity to web traffic, lead generation, sales, and reputation surveys
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a PR measurement dashboard: tracking coverage volume, share of voice, sentiment, key message penetration, and website traffic from earned media, then interpreting the data to recommend strategy adjustments
setup_requirements: Media monitoring tool (Google Alerts, free Mention alternative)

review.value: AMEC's seven principles for PR measurement: goal setting is prerequisite, measure outcomes not outputs, measure impact on organizational performance, quantitative and qualitative methods are both needed, AVE is not valid, social media measurement is essential, transparency and replicability are paramount

Advertising Value Equivalency (calculating earned media value as if it were purchased ad space) is rejected by the Barcelona Principles because editorial coverage is not equivalent to advertising and the calculation inflates PR value without measuring actual business impact

questions.question: Describe the PESO model and explain why modern PR professionals must integrate all four media types rather than focusing solely on earned media.
questions.answer: PESO stands for Paid (advertising, sponsored content), Earned (media coverage, reviews), Shared (social media, user content), and Owned (website, blog, email). Modern PR must integrate all four because: (1) Earned media alone is unpredictable and uncontrollable; you cannot guarantee coverage. (2) Owned media gives you direct audience access without media intermediaries. (3) Shared media amplifies earned and owned content through audience participation. (4) Paid media ensures visibility when organic reach is insufficient. Integration creates a mutually reinforcing system where each type supports the others.

rubric.key: Correctly defines all four PESO components
Explains why earned media alone is insufficient

questions.question: What makes an effective media pitch, and identify two common mistakes that cause journalists to delete pitches without reading them.
questions.answer: An effective media pitch is brief (under 200 words), personalized to the journalist's beat and recent coverage, opens with a clear news hook, and makes the next step easy (offering an interview, exclusive data, or visual assets). Two common mistakes: (1) Sending generic, mass-email pitches that show no knowledge of the journalist's work or publication, signaling that the PR person did not research before sending. (2) Leading with the company or product rather than the story; journalists care about stories their readers want, not about your product announcement. A pitch that reads like a press release instead of a story proposal gets deleted immediately.

questions.question: Define newsjacking and describe one ethical guideline that prevents it from being perceived as opportunistic.
questions.answer: Newsjacking is the practice of quickly inserting your brand's expertise into a trending news story to earn media coverage and visibility. Ethical guideline: only newsjack stories where you have genuine expertise to contribute. If your brand has relevant knowledge, data, or perspective that adds value to the conversation, newsjacking is a public service (providing expert commentary). If your brand has no genuine connection to the story and is simply exploiting attention, it appears opportunistic and damages credibility. The test is: would a journalist genuinely benefit from your input on this story?

rubric.key: Identifies genuine expertise as the ethical test

questions.question: Explain why the Barcelona Principles reject AVE (Advertising Value Equivalency) as a valid PR measurement.
questions.answer: AVE calculates earned media value by measuring the column inches or airtime of coverage and multiplying by the equivalent advertising rate. The Barcelona Principles reject it because: (1) Editorial coverage is not equivalent to advertising; a news article carries different credibility, engagement, and impact than a paid ad in the same space. (2) AVE inflates PR value without measuring actual audience behavior, awareness change, or business impact. (3) AVE does not account for message quality (was the coverage positive or negative?), message penetration (did the key messages appear?), or audience action (did anyone do anything as a result?). It measures visibility, not effectiveness.

rubric.key: Identifies at least two specific reasons for rejection
Connects to the need for outcome-based measurement

major_assignments.title: Integrated PR Campaign
major_assignments.description: Develop and execute a comprehensive PR campaign using the PESO model for a real organization or cause. The campaign must include earned media outreach, owned content creation, shared media strategy, and a measurement framework aligned with the Barcelona Principles.
objectives: Design a PESO-integrated campaign with clear objectives and stakeholder focus
Create professional PR materials including press releases, pitches, and owned content
Execute media outreach with a targeted list and tracking system
Measure campaign results using Barcelona Principles-aligned methodology
deliverables: Campaign strategy document (8-10 pages) with PESO integration plan
Press release and three personalized media pitches for different outlet types
Owned content package: blog post, social media content plan, and email announcement
Media list (minimum 20 contacts) with beat research and personalization notes
Measurement report using Barcelona Principles with results analysis and recommendations

criteria: Campaign objectives are SMART and aligned with organizational goals
PESO integration is genuine, not just listing tactics in four categories
Stakeholder analysis informs messaging strategy
Timeline is realistic with clear milestones
Press release follows professional format and includes a genuine news hook
Pitches are personalized and compelling for their target journalists
Owned content is high-quality and strategically aligned
All materials are publication-ready
Media list demonstrates genuine research into journalist beats and interests
Outreach is tracked and documented
Social media content is created and scheduled
Campaign demonstrates professional execution
Metrics align with Barcelona Principles
Outcomes are measured, not just outputs
Analysis provides genuine insight beyond numbers
Recommendations are grounded in measurement data
Strategic PESO-integrated campaign demonstrating modern PR thinking
Professional-quality PR materials ready for real-world use
Rigorous execution with documented outreach and tracking
Barcelona Principles-aligned measurement with actionable insights

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on PR strategy and media relations

weekly_exercises.description: Weekly pitching, writing, and media relations exercises

major_assignment.description: Integrated PR campaign with PESO model

midterm_examination.description: Practical examination on media relations and PR writing

final_examination.description: Cumulative examination including live pitch and measurement analysis

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Guest speaker sessions with working journalists cannot be rescheduled. More than 3 absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Press releases and pitches submitted late cannot be reviewed in time for feedback cycles.
course_policies.collaboration: Media research may be shared among peers. All pitches, press releases, and strategy documents are individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All PR materials must be original. Media contacts must be real and accurately researched. Fabricating media coverage or journalist responses is academic fraud.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need access to media monitoring tools (Google Alerts, free alternatives), PR writing platforms, and social media for monitoring. Muck Rack educational access is provided.
